Q1: I tried to keep things somewhat organized, but honestly it was a struggle with limited resources. I usually set aside time in the evenings after work to study, maybe three hours a day when I could manage it. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found myself needing to physically write things out or practice problems by hand rather than just reading. I'd often spread my materials across my small desk and work through concepts step by step, which took time but helped things stick.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I aimed for consistency, studying most evenings, but some weeks were heavier than others. When deadlines approached, I'd push harder and study longer, but during quieter weeks I might ease up a bit. Being 25 and managing limited resources means unexpected expenses or responsibilities can throw off even the best plans.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due or when I needed to check if new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion or assignment and that would prompt me to go in. I also logged in when I felt uncertain about what was expected, just to make sure I wasn't missing something important. It wasn't always about active studying—sometimes just checking in to stay aware.
Q4: It took a while, but eventually checking the LMS became part of my evening routine. After dinner, I'd open my laptop and just see what was there, even if I wasn't planning to do heavy work. The CBT session I had actually helped me build better habits around this—it made me realize that small consistent actions matter. Once I connected logging in with my existing evening wind-down, it felt less like a chore.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause and rewind when something didn't click. I watched a lot of video content—over two and a half hours worth—because visual demonstrations helped me understand concepts better. The discussion posts were also useful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. When I could actually interact with the material rather than just read it, I retained more.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped or delayed the lengthy reading PDFs, especially when they felt dense and theoretical. With my kinesthetic learning style, just reading pages of text doesn't stick well for me. I also minimized time on some optional quizzes when I was stretched thin—my assignment completion rate wasn't great at 48%, and I had to prioritize what would count most. Limited internet at times also meant I couldn't always access everything when I wanted to.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to what I'd be tested on or what would help me complete assignments. I also valued activities that let me actively do something rather than passively consume information. If I could practice a skill or work through a problem, that felt productive. Activities that seemed disconnected from outcomes or too abstract just felt like time I couldn't afford to spend.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd cut when time got tight. Required activities had a weight to them, a consequence if I didn't complete them. Optional ones felt like nice-to-haves that I genuinely wanted to do but often couldn't justify when I was already behind on required work. There was always this guilt about skipping them, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also kept a physical notebook where I'd work through problems by hand—that kinesthetic practice was essential for me. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or free resources online that explained concepts differently, and none of that showed up in the LMS.
Q10: Many times I was working through problems in my notebook or reviewing printed materials at a coffee shop where I didn't have reliable internet. I'd spend hours practicing calculations or rewriting notes to understand them better, but the LMS wouldn't capture any of that. There were also times I'd discuss concepts with a friend in person, working through ideas together, which was real learning but invisible online.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then continue working with that material offline for days. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could. The LMS would show one view or download, but that single access might represent hours of later study.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when multiple courses had overlapping deadlines. I felt this moderate anxiety building up, and my motivation wasn't great to begin with. Financial stress from having limited resources also crept in—worrying about bills made it hard to focus on coursework. During those times, even logging in felt like a burden, and I'd just do the bare minimum.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS use. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, I'd log in less frequently or just skim materials instead of engaging deeply. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections—I'd procrastinate on things I didn't understand rather than face them. My task completion being at 65% reflects those times when I just couldn't push through.
Q14: Honestly, yes. There were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out, not really absorbing anything. I'd be physically present on the LMS but mentally somewhere else, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. Those sessions felt wasted, but I counted them as study time anyway.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially complex assignments that felt overwhelming. When my resilience was low, I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow often brought new challenges. The anxiety made some tasks feel bigger than they were, so I'd avoid them until the deadline forced my hand. Looking back, the CBT session helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still hard.
Q16: Deadlines basically drove most of my LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—logging in more, watching videos I'd been putting off, participating in discussions to meet requirements. Without deadlines, I'd drift and lose focus. It wasn't ideal, but that external pressure was often the only thing that got me moving when motivation was low.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got good feedback on my assignments—and my assignment scores were actually pretty decent at 87—it motivated me to keep going. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I felt lost about whether I was on the right track. Positive feedback after the CBT session especially helped my mood and made me more willing to engage with the course.
Q18: The course structure with 12 online courses was honestly a lot to manage. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize, but the sheer volume sometimes felt overwhelming. I appreciated when the course laid out expectations clearly, because ambiguity just added to my anxiety. The structure worked when I could see the path forward, but fell apart when things piled up.
Q19: Having video content was definitely easier for my learning style—I could engage more actively by pausing and practicing. But some aspects made engagement harder, like when materials were only available in text-heavy formats or when discussions required responses at specific times I couldn't always make. Limited flexibility in how I could demonstrate learning also frustrated me sometimes.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might watch videos multiple times, read through materials repeatedly, and post in discussions trying to grasp concepts, but still not feel confident enough to submit work. It could also be anxiety—being active feels productive, but actually submitting something feels too risky. I've felt that fear of being judged on my work.
Q21: This actually resonates with me somewhat. My LMS engagement was rated low, but I still managed a Merit grade and decent exam scores. A lot of my learning happened offline—in my notebook, with downloaded materials, through practice problems I worked through by hand. The LMS doesn't capture that hands-on work that kinesthetic learners like me rely on. Performance isn't always reflected in clicks and logins.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns—like when someone logs in, how long they spend on videos, whether they meet deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning that happens. They can't measure understanding, effort, or the personal circumstances affecting someone's participation. A log shows behavior but not the why behind it or the learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, connecting them to things I already know, and wanting to master them rather than just pass. True engagement feels like curiosity and investment, even when it's hard. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about whether my mind is really in it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with my notebook, working through problems until they clicked. It wouldn't capture the conversations with friends where we helped each other understand difficult concepts. It also wouldn't show my emotional journey—the anxiety I managed, the impact of the CBT session on my outlook, or how financial stress affected my capacity to engage. My real story is much more than my login data.
